story:
Ellen Gable, an advertising executive, comes home later than usual to her husband, Dale, who has cooked spaghetti, meatballs, and garlic bread. They swap quick notes about their days—he jokes about the flood of kids needing shots and camp physicals—and sit down to eat. Mid-bite, Ellen gasps. At the far end of the table, she sees a leprechaun eating and praising Dale’s cooking in a heavy Irish brogue: “This is delicious. Your husband’s a good cook.” Dale, startled, insists there’s no one there and no extra plate. The leprechaun calmly tells Ellen that only she can see him.

He introduces himself as Declan and addresses her as “your highness.” He has come, he says, to tell her she has been chosen to be queen of the leprechauns. The late king, Shannon O’Connell, reigned for 545 years and had many descendants, but none suited to rule. After “extensive genealogical research,” the leprechauns discovered that Ellen’s great-great-great-great-great grandfather was a leprechaun and that Ellen has the qualities of a leader. Ellen protests that leprechauns aren’t real. Declan tells her to pinch herself and points out that mortals only see leprechauns when leprechauns allow it.

“What does a queen do?” Ellen asks. Declan replies that first she must choose a king and then make decisions and rule. She quips that she already has a king—her husband—one king is enough. Declan is apologetic but firm: Dale is not a leprechaun, so he cannot be her king. Watching his wife speak to an empty chair, Dale grows alarmed and suggests the emergency room. Ellen assures him she’s fine. Declan, meanwhile, finishes the invisible meal—“That was delicious”—and asks if she’s ready to go to the leprechaun castle. Ellen laughs off the idea: she isn’t going anywhere. Declan reminds her she can’t rule from here. She turns toward Dale, half amused: she wishes he could hear this conversation—this talk of her “leprechaun castle”—and then she disappears.

Time shifts. Dale sits on their young son Jamie’s bed at bedtime and tells him a story: Mommy went to the land of the leprechauns and became their queen, and she and the leprechauns “lived happily ever after.” Jamie, comforted but curious, asks if Mommy will always be queen and whether she will ever come home. Dale doesn’t know. It’s been six years, he says, and that is a long time. He kisses Jamie good night, goes to the living room, sits on the couch, leans back, and sobs. “Ellen, where are you?”
